title: To Jim
content:
혻혻혻혻I gaze upon my son once more,
혻혻혻혻With eyes and heart that tire,
혻혻혻혻As solemnly he stands before
혻혻혻혻The screen drawn round the fire;
혻혻혻혻With hands behind clasped hand in hand,
혻혻혻혻Now loosely and now fast,
혻혻혻혻Just as his fathers used to stand
혻혻혻혻For generations past.

혻혻혻혻A fair and slight and childish form,
혻혻혻혻And big brown thoughtful eyes,
혻혻혻혻God help him! for a life of storm
혻혻혻혻And stress before him lies:
혻혻혻혻A wanderer and a gipsy wild,
혻혻혻혻I占퐒e learnt the world and know,
혻혻혻혻For I was such another child,
혻혻혻혻Ah, many years ago!

혻혻혻혻But in those dreamy eyes of him
혻혻혻혻There is no hint of doubt,
혻혻혻혻I wish that you could tell me, Jim,
혻혻혻혻The things you dream about.
혻혻혻혻Dream on, my son, that all is true
혻혻혻혻And things not what they seem,
혻혻혻혻占폯will be a bitter day for you
혻혻혻혻When wakened from your dream.

혻혻혻혻You are a child of field and flood,
혻혻혻혻But with the gipsy strains
혻혻혻혻A strong Norwegian sailor占퐏 blood
혻혻혻혻Is running through your veins.
혻혻혻혻Be true, and slander never stings,
혻혻혻혻Be straight, and all may frown,
혻혻혻혻You占퐇l have the strength to grapple things
혻혻혻혻That dragged your father down.

혻혻혻혻These lines I write with bitter tears
혻혻혻혻And failing heart and hand,
혻혻혻혻But you will read in after years,
혻혻혻혻And you will understand:
혻혻혻혻You占퐇l hear the slander of the crowd,
혻혻혻혻They占퐇l whisper tales of shame,
혻혻혻혻But days will come when you占퐇l be proud
혻혻혻혻To bear your father占퐏 name.

혻혻혻혻But oh! beware of bitterness
혻혻혻혻When you are wronged, my lad,
혻혻혻혻I wish I had the faith in men
혻혻혻혻And women that I had!
혻혻혻혻占폯is better far (for I have felt
혻혻혻혻The sadness in my song)
혻혻혻혻To trust all men and still be wronged
혻혻혻혻Than to trust none and wrong.

혻혻혻혻Be generous and still do good
혻혻혻혻And banish while you live
혻혻혻혻The spectre of ingratitude
혻혻혻혻That haunts the ones who give.
혻혻혻혻But if the crisis comes at length
혻혻혻혻That your future might be marred,
혻혻혻혻Strike hard, my son, with all your strength!
혻혻혻혻For your own self占퐏 sake, strike hard!
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
